one_line_takeaway: A qualitative MAESTRO-based threat model of Google's A2A agent protocol that catalogs ten threats (notably prompt-injection-poisoned AgentCards) and maps each to concrete but largely conventional mitigations and server-hardening best practices, with no empirical evaluation.
key_finding: The distinctive AI-native risk highlighted is the 'Poisoned AgentCard': because AgentCard content is auto-ingested and fed to a foundation model during discovery/planning, prompt-injection payloads hidden in card fields can hijack a consuming agent's goals, exfiltrate data, or bypass its safety controls — so AgentCard content must be treated as untrusted input requiring sanitization/CSP/schema validation. The paper also argues A2A and MCP are complementary (A2A = horizontal agent-to-agent coordination; MCP = vertical agent-to-tool/data integration) and warns that cross-protocol boundaries create new attack surface (e.g., an A2A auth flaw enabling unauthorized MCP access).
core_contribution: A conceptual security analysis of Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol for multi-agent AI systems. The paper's own contribution is: (1) applying the MAESTRO seven-layer threat-modeling framework to A2A deployments to enumerate a concrete set of A2A-specific threats; (2) proposing per-threat mitigation strategies; and (3) providing implementation best practices for hardening A2A servers/clients, plus an analysis of how A2A and Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP) can be combined securely. It is a position/guidance paper, not an experimental study; no system is built and no measurements are reported. The authors accompany it with a public repo of secure A2A coding examples (github.com/kenhuangus/a2a-secure-coding-examples).
threat_model: Adversaries in an open, multi-vendor, federated agent ecosystem where agents discover each other via publicly hosted AgentCards (/.well-known/agent.json) and exchange declarative Tasks over HTTP/JSON-RPC/SSE. The paper enumerates ten A2A-specific threats mapped to MAESTRO layers: (1) Agent Card Spoofing via forged/typosquatted cards (layers 3,4); (2) A2A Task Replay of captured tasks/send requests (3,2); (3) Message Schema Violation causing injection/DoS (2); (4) A2A Server Impersonation via DNS spoofing (4); (5) Cross-Agent Task Escalation with forged credentials (7,3); (6) Artifact Tampering (2,3); (7) Insider Threat / logging evasion via Task-state manipulation (6,3); (8) Supply-Chain Attack via A2A dependencies (4,6); (9) Authentication/Identity threats — forged/stolen/replayed JWTs, weak validation (3,4,6,7); (10) Poisoned AgentCard — prompt-injection payloads embedded in AgentCard fields (skill id/name/description/tags/examples) that hijack a consuming agent's foundation model (layers 1,2). Two case studies (collaborative document processing; distributed data analysis) walk the threats layer-by-layer, adding model-inversion and data-poisoning concerns.
limitations: No empirical validation: not a single threat is demonstrated, exploited, or measured, and no mitigation is tested for effectiveness or overhead — claims rest entirely on reasoning. The threat list and mitigations are largely a re-application of standard web/API security controls (TLS, mTLS, JWT hardening, nonces, RBAC, SBOM) to the A2A setting; few are genuinely novel to agentic AI beyond the AgentCard-poisoning vector. Heavy reliance on the authors' own prior artifacts (MAESTRO [2], A2A threat-modeling blog [3], MCP security paper [21]) for the framework. Targets a fast-moving, then-nascent protocol (arXiv v2, May 2025), so specifics like tasks.send method names and AgentCard schema may drift. No prioritization/quantified risk scoring beyond coarse High/Medium-High likelihood-impact labels in the two case studies.
